Yet it remains unclear whether these distinctions shape solely the formation of short-term fertility intentions or also their realisation as births. Research into the significance of cohabitation has examined how intentions form without considering their translation into behaviour, whereas research into the intention–realisation gap has addressed cohabitation as a single, undifferentiated category. Employing data from two panel rounds of the Czech Generations and Gender Survey, each comprising a baseline and three-year follow-up (round 1: 2005 and 2008; round 2: 2020–2022 and 2025), binary and multinomial logistic regression was applied to examine whether the importance of partnership type differed at these two stages for women and men aged 18–44 in coresidential partnerships. The study distinguished married individuals, cohabitants who intended to marry and those without such intentions. Partnership type strongly differentiates the formation of fertility intentions: cohabitants who intend to marry are most likely to plan a child, whereas those without marriage intentions are substantially less likely to do so. These differences appeared to narrow over time (albeit marginally), in line with the normalisation of cohabitation. In contrast, no evidence was found that the legal or intentional character of the union is independently associated with realisation, once parity and intention certainty were accounted for. Overall, partnership type structures selection into fertility intentions, whereas their realisation appears to depend more on life-course position and motivational commitment than on institutional form.

title: Language Development: Input, Multilingualism, and the Literacy Environment - A Blue Blocks Montessori School Home Observation Study, Hyderabad
authors: Pavan Goyal, Sreemoyee Chakraborty, Raunak Sharma
abstract: The present study investigates the home language environment of 99 children from Blue Blocks Montessori School, Hyderabad, for the period January-February 2026, focusing on three interrelated areas of interest: (i) amount and quality of adults’ linguistic input, (ii) type of the multilingual environment, and (iii) nature of home literacy environment. Based on the Montessori principle of the sensitive period for language (Montessori, 1949; Montessori, 1946) and recent research concerning language learning, bilingualism and early literacy (Hart & Risley, 1995; Cummins, 2000; Neuman & Dickinson, 2001), we analysed parental reports on story time and song singing, narrations of activities, conversation richness, and the variety and distribution of sources of input and information within a multilingual context, as well as reading practices and presence of books. Key findings include: a majority of children receive more than one hour of extended adult narrative daily; 88% live in multilingual households; mother is the primary language-input source in 43% of families; structured one-person-one-language practice is rarely maintained; mealtime conversation is predominantly child-led; and home literacy environments are moderately rich but with significant variability in book quantity and dedicated reading infrastructure. Parent reflective data illuminate a community in active, joyful language development. Implications for Montessori language pedagogy and home-school language partnership are discussed.

title: Electoral systems shape the entry and entrenchment of extremist parties
authors: Chanuwas Aswamenakul, Tyler Marghetis, Brad LeVeck, Paul E. Smaldino
abstract: Representative democracies aspire to represent voters’ viewpoints through their elected officials. At the same time, they may try to minimize extremist viewpoints that can destabilize democracy itself. Here we show that electoral systems navigate this tension by trading off between two vulnerabilities: extremist entry and extremist entrenchment. Using computational models, we investigate how electoral systems respond to the emergence of ideological extremism arising either through the introduction of a new party or through the gradual radicalization of an established party. Systems that more accurately represent public opinion are more permissive to new parties, including extremist ones. Conversely, systems that suppress extremist entry can also protect incumbent parties as they drift toward increasingly extreme and unrepresentative positions. Electoral systems therefore differ not only in how well they represent voters or how many parties they sustain, but in the vulnerabilities they create: institutional openness increases the risk of extremist entry, while institutional inertia increases the risk of extremist entrenchment. Democracies thus face a fundamental design challenge: The same institutional features that protect a democracy from one extremist risk also expose it to another.

title: How Americans Spend their Time Online
authors: David Lazer, Burak Ozturan, Christo Wilson, David Choffnes, Cassidy Waldrip, Hsiu Chi,Lu, John Wihbey
abstract: We examine where people spend their time online, analyzing desktop browsing data collected from the National Internet Observatory (NIO). Key findings include: ● A few platforms dominate where we spend our time. The top 10 platforms captured about half (49%) of all online time, with Gmail at number one with 16.4%. ● A tiny number of companies dominate where we spend time. Most user attention is funneled through a handful of corporations. Alphabet (Google’s parent company) alone accounts for 35% of total browsing time in our sample, followed by Meta (8.9%), Microsoft (5.1%), Yahoo (4.3%), and Amazon (3.0%). These five companies capture more than half of all time Americans spend online on desktop. ● Americans spend half their online time on just two things: social media (25.3%) and email (24.9%). ● AI is already bigger than news. Large Language Model (LLM) tools, such as ChatGPT, now account for 2.9% of total online time, while news is only 2.5%. ● The Web is highly age-segregated. Users over 65 spend 36.7% of their online time on email and 14% on Facebook, versus 11% and 2%, respectively, for users under 30. Older people are still using Yahoo and AOL for email (9%, 2%, respectively), both of which barely register among younger users. The oldest cohort spends 4.7% of their time on news sites, far more than the youngest cohort (1.2%). In contrast, users under 30 spend 13% of their time on YouTube, 9% on Google apps (Docs, Drive, Meet, Classroom, and other Google services excluding Gmail, Search, and YouTube), and 3% on ChatGPT, as compared to 6%, 3%, and <1%, respectively, among the oldest cohort.

title: Youth Unemployment and Mismatch in the Turkish Labour Market
abstract: This paper analyses youth unemployment and education–job mismatch in Turkey using individual-level data from the Turkish Statistical Institute(TÜİK) Labour Force Statistics for 2015. The youth (15–24) unemployment rate stands at 18.5%, more than double the adult (25–64) rate of 8.8%, with a pronounced gender gap: female youth face a 22.2% unemployment rate against 16.5% for males. Unemployment is highest among college-educated youth (29.5%), alongside high labour-force participation (75.1%) in this group. We construct two qualification-mismatch indicators for employed youth. Vertical mismatch - education level relative to the occupational modal - affects 53% of employed college graduates and 49% of vocational high school graduates, who work in occupations where the typical worker has lower formal education. Horizontal mismatch, field of education relative to occupation, is estimable for the 27.5% of employed youth with a field-of-education code; unmatched rates are highest for health and welfare (81%), humanities, arts, and social sciences (78%), and science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (68%) graduates. Logistic regression models confirm that female youth face significantly higher unemployment odds (OR = 1.31), that higher education is associated with greater overeducation risk (OR = 8.3 per education step), and that horizontal mismatch varies substantially across fields of study and regions. These findings are consistent with persistent misalignments between formal education and observed job allocation in Turkey, with implications for vocational education, graduate employment policy, and the school-to-work transition.

title: From Participation to Influence: The Role of Digital Civic Engagement in Strengthening Democratic Accountability and Active Citizenship among Nigerian Youth
abstract: Nigeria’s youth population is the largest on the African continent, yet formal civic participation among people aged 18 to 35 has fallen sharply even as their digital connectivity has grown. This paper examines how digital civic engagement platforms, civic technology initiatives, and online advocacy are reshaping the relationship between young Nigerians and democratic governance. The study employs systematic secondary data analysis, drawing on Afrobarometer survey data (Rounds 6 to 9), World Bank Governance Indicators, INEC electoral records, UNDP governance reports, Open Government Partnership assessments, and peer-reviewed literature published between 2015 and 2024. Results show that social media usage among Nigerian youth rose from 11.2 percent in 2015 to 53.6 percent by 2024, while formal voter turnout among the same group declined from 53.7 percent in 2011 to 29.4 percent in 2023. Digital platforms produced concrete accountability outcomes in notable cases: the #EndSARS movement of 2020 led directly to the disbandment of the Special Anti-Robbery Squad, and civic technology platforms such as BudgIT show statistically significant improvements in public project completion rates in communities using their monitoring tools. Comparative analysis with Ghana, South Africa, Kenya, and Senegal reveals that Nigeria underperforms on governance effectiveness relative to peers despite having the largest digital youth population on the continent. Five barriers explain this gap: institutional trust deficits, digital literacy shortfalls, high internet costs, fear of online surveillance, and a widely felt disconnect between digital activism and tangible policy outcomes. The paper argues that digital tools are necessary but not on their own sufficient for democratic transformation, and puts forward eight evidence-based policy recommendations for government, civil society organisations, and international development partners.

title: When the Target Domain Changes: AI-Mediated Construct Drift in High-Stakes English Language Assessment
abstract: High-stakes English proficiency tests treat standardized, unaided performance as evidence for score interpretations about academic English proficiency. This interpretation remains meaningful, but as target language use domains increasingly involve generative AI, the extrapolation from unaided test performance to academic communicative readiness becomes less self-evident. This conceptual validity argument reframes AI as a score-interpretation problem in high-stakes language testing, not only an operational issue of scoring, feedback, security, or misconduct. Synthesizing current literature in three uneven layers, the paper shows that most work treats AI as assessment infrastructure, while far less theorizes its implications for construct validity and extrapolation warrants. It defines AI-mediated construct drift as the misalignment that arises when communicative abilities required in the target domain change through AI mediation while test constructs remain anchored to an unaided-performance model. It proposes bounded AI mediation as a validity-oriented design principle: a standardized condition in which all test takers access the same institutionally controlled AI assistant, with predefined assistance boundaries, logged interactions, and tasks that distinguish comprehension support from answer generation. The paper argues that score interpretations should be narrowed and supplemented when used to support claims about AI-mediated academic communication.

title: The Role of FinTech in Promoting Green Investment in Emerging Economies
abstract: This study examines the relationship between financial technology development and green investment flows across 12 emerging economies from 2015 to 2024. Using fixed effects panel data regression, the findings indicate that FinTech development is significantly associated with increased green investment (β = 0.347, p < 0.01), with threshold effects observed at moderate levels of technological infrastructure. The analysis reveals that mobile payment penetration and digital lending platforms serve as the primary channels through which FinTech influences green capital allocation. Policy implications for emerging market regulators seeking to leverage digital finance for sustainability goals are discussed.

title: Barriers to Digital Transformation of SMEs in Transition Economies: Implications for Green Growth
abstract: This paper investigates barriers preventing small and medium enterprises from adopting digital technologies for green business models in transition economies. A survey of 385 SME owners across Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan, and Kyrgyzstan was conducted. Factor analysis identifies five barrier dimensions, and logistic regression reveals that financial constraints and human capital deficits are the strongest predictors of non-adoption. Recommendations for targeted policy interventions are provided

title: Inclusive Climate Science and Policy Require New Infrastructures for Ethical Data Management
abstract: Workshop contribution: Inclusive Open Science – From Global Asymmetries to Pluriversal Design (14 July 2025, hybrid: online and Einstein Center Digital Future, Berlin), organised by Michelle Christensen and Fernanda Beigel. Complex global societal challenges demand inclusive, interdisciplinary collaboration across research communities and non-academic stakeholders, as well as new data infrastructures to integrate and archive fragmented information. Efforts to generate open datasets are particularly challenging in anthropology and related fields: beyond logistical and technical issues of accessing data and standardisation, ethical challenges arise when working with small-scale and Indigenous communities. Drawing on our research on traditional food production and environmental change, we show how integrating diverse, small-scale datasets can inform climate policy and insights on food security. We highlight risks of data loss, the need for culturally-grounded metadata standards, and the importance of Indigenous Data Sovereignty. To balance data accessibility with local priorities, we advocate designing databases and data infrastructures that are grounded in both FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) and CARE (Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility, Ethics) principles from the outset. Doing so is essential not only for more equitable and policy-relevant Open Science, but also to support sustained collaboration among researchers, Indigenous communities, and policymakers.

title: The Cadrefication of Medicine: Administrative Absorption and Professional Alienation in China’s Grassroots Healthcare
abstract: China’s county-level healthcare system is increasingly governed by administrative performance indicators, transforming clinical practice into bureaucratic compliance. Based on in-depth interviews with 12 frontline doctors in Henan Province and an analysis of policy texts from 1986–2026, this paper proposes the concept of “cadre-ized healthcare.” It argues that Chinese physicians have been institutionally embedded as “technical cadres” within a dual evaluation system prioritizing political quality over professional competence. Facing intense administrative pressure amid resource scarcity and demographic aging, doctors have developed systematic “self-protection” strategies—including defensive medicine, medical record embellishment, systemic rule evasion, and professional exit. These behaviors mirror the “self-informing” tactics observed among grassroots cadres. Integrating theories of professional autonomy, street-level bureaucracy, and the Red Queen effect, the study demonstrates how performance governance converts professional actors into administrative assessment targets. Furthermore, it reveals that AI-driven regulatory countermeasures have not resolved these dilemmas but instead triggered a dynamic arms race between regulators and practitioners. This research contributes to medical sociology, cadre system studies, and public management theory by offering a new analytical lens for understanding governance crises in China’s professional service sectors under the cadre state logic.

title: Generative Artificial Intelligence and Gender: A Systematic Scoping Review from a Sociotechnical Perspective
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence has been rapidly incorporated into educational, professional, communicative, healthcare, and institutional contexts, creating opportunities but also new risks in terms of gender equality. This study presents a systematic scoping review of generative artificial intelligence and gender from a sociotechnical perspective. Following a PRISMA-based procedure, 1,372 bibliographic records were identified and, after the screening and eligibility process, 108 studies published between 2022 and 2026 were included. The results were organized into six dimensions: visual and representational biases; language, ChatGPT, and gender performativity; digital violence, deepfakes, and psychosocial harm; gaps in use, trust, anxiety, and well-being; education and sociocritical literacy; and governance, health, and work. The discussion shows that these dimensions are articulated through cross-cutting tensions: visibility versus equality, technical neutrality versus sociocultural reproduction, automated efficiency versus evaluative bias, technological innovation versus psychosocial harm, access versus critical appropriation, individual anxiety versus structural vulnerability, and instrumental use versus sociocritical literacy. The review shows that generative AI does not operate as a neutral tool, but rather as a sociotechnical infrastructure capable of reproducing, amplifying, or reconfiguring inequalities through images, texts, interactions, automated decisions, and emerging forms of epistemic authority. The article concludes that the analysis of generative AI and gender requires the integration of technical, social, psychosocial, educational, and normative dimensions, as well as responses based on critical literacy, human oversight, gender-sensitive regulation, and intersectional approaches.

title: Toward a Multilevel Model of Algorithmic Sociocognitive Mediation in Human-AI Interaction
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence is increasingly embedded in everyday digital environments, reshaping the conditions under which individuals perceive, interpret, validate, and respond to social information. However, current research on human-AI interaction remains fragmented across cognitive, behavioral, organizational, communicative, and sociotechnical approaches, limiting the development of integrated explanatory frameworks. This article proposes the Algorithmic Sociocognitive Mediation Model (ASM) as a multilevel theoretical framework designed to explain how algorithmic-generative systems participate in the reorganization of perception, attention, interpretation, cognition, and behavior. The model conceptualizes algorithmic mediation as an adaptive process emerging from the interaction among four interrelated dimensions: sociotechnical structures, configurations of human-AI interaction, recursive and coadaptive feedback processes, and sociocognitive consequences. It argues that the effects of generative AI cannot be reduced to technological exposure, individual adoption, or functional automation, but must be understood through intermediate states of mediation such as algorithmic trust, cognitive delegation, attentional overload, functional dependence, critical monitoring, and behavioral regulation. The ASM also highlights the role of algorithmic legitimacy, institutional context, decisional autonomy, personalization, and human oversight in modulating these effects. Rather than presenting generative AI as either an inherently augmenting or disruptive technology, the model identifies the configurational conditions under which algorithmic systems may support cognitive extension, self-regulation, and decision-making, or alternatively foster uncritical delegation, reduced epistemic responsibility, fatigue, dependence, and diminished autonomy. The article concludes by outlining theoretical, methodological, and normative implications for future empirical research on generative AI within complex sociotechnical ecosystems.
